title: Character-Level Language Models
concept: Character-level models predict letter-by-letter; they have tiny vocabularies and never output OOV tokens, but struggle with long-term grammar.

exercises.prompt: What basic unit does a Character-Level Language Model predict?Level:100-word paragraphs at a time
exercises.explain: A character-level language model processes text at the level of individual characters (letters, numbers, punctuation) rather than whole words.

cards.title: Zooming In on Letters
cards.body: Have you ever wondered how your phone autocomplete knows how to spell out weird words, or how an AI can invent realistic-sounding fantasy names? Instead of thinking in whole words, some models read and write one single letter at a time!

cards.title: What is a Character-Level Model?
cards.body: A Character-Level Language Model is an AI that reads and predicts text letter-by-letter (or character-by-character) instead of word-by-word. Because it only needs to know the alphabet, punctuation, and spaces, its vocabulary is tiny, and it never gets stuck on unfamiliar words.

cards.title: The Letter-by-Letter Copyist
cards.body: Think of a writer who only knows how to copy letters one by one, rather than writing whole words. While they can easily spell any strange name you make up, they might lose track of how a sentence started by the time they reach the end of a long paragraph because they are focused on individual letters.

cards.title: Predicting Letter by Letter
cards.body: If you train a character-level model on English, and type "c-a-t", it predicts "s" or a space next. If you type a made-up word like "Z-o-p-h-i-a", it doesn't break down or crash; it just predicts the next likely letters based on spelling patterns it has seen.

cards.title: Can They Learn Grammar?
cards.body: People often think that because these models work with letters, they can't understand English grammar. In reality, they can learn grammar, but they have to work much harder because they must learn how letters form words, and how words form sentences, all from scratch!

cards.title: Letters vs. Words
cards.body: Let's compare how character-level models process text versus standard word-level models.

left.text: Sees "c-a-t". Tiny vocabulary (alphabet). Never gets stuck on new words, but takes many steps to read a sentence.

right.text: Sees "cat". Huge vocabulary. Fast at reading sentences, but gets confused by new or misspelled words.

exercises.prompt: Which of the following is a key advantage of character-level models?

options.text: They have a tiny vocabulary of just letters and symbols.
They require millions of words in their vocabulary dictionary.
They cannot output spaces or punctuation marks.
They are much faster at processing long books than word models.

exercises.explain: Because character-level models build words out of individual characters, they only need a tiny vocabulary (usually around 100 to 256 characters, including letters, numbers, and punctuation).

exercises.prompt: True or False: Character-level models are better at remembering long-term context than word-level models.
exercises.explain: Character-level models process text letter-by-letter. This means a single sentence takes many steps to process, making it harder for them to remember long-term context compared to word-level models.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing term about character-level models.
exercises.sentence: Character models never run into blank errors because they can build any word letter-by-letter.
exercises.explain: Because a character-level model only needs to know the basic alphabet and symbols to build any word, it never runs into 'out-of-vocabulary' (OOV) errors.

exercises.prompt:
Identify which code snippet represents a Character-Level Model approach.

Snippet A:
`input_sequence = ['h', 'e', 'l']` -> `predict_next_char(input_sequence)` -> outputs `'l'`

Snippet B:
`input_sequence = ['hello', 'world']` -> `predict_next_word(input_sequence)` -> outputs `'!'`
exercises.explain: The first snippet is character-by-character because it updates its state and predicts the next letter ('e', then 'l', then 'l') individually. The second snippet works with whole word tokens.

exercises.prompt: Why do character-level language models struggle to write long, coherent stories?

options.text: They cannot spell newly invented words.
They easily lose track of the overall sentence structure.
Their vocabulary size becomes too massive to manage.
They require a dictionary of every possible word.

exercises.explain: Because character-level models process text one letter at a time, their 'context window' gets filled up quickly with individual letters and spaces. This makes it difficult for them to remember facts or keep grammar consistent over long sentences compared to models that process whole words.
